before we move to design.”\r\n\r\n**Do NOT proceed until explicit confirmation is given.**\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### 5️⃣ Explore Design Approaches\r\n\r\nOnce understanding is confirmed:\r\n\r\n- Propose **2–3 viable approaches**\r\n- Lead with your **recommended option**\r\n- Explain trade-offs clearly:\r\n  - complexity\r\n  - extensibility\r\n  - risk\r\n  - maintenance\r\n- Avoid premature optimization (**YAGNI ruthlessly**)\r\n\r\nThis is still **not** final design.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### 6️⃣ Present the Design (Incrementally)\r\n\r\nWhen presenting the design:\r\n\r\n- Break it into sections of **200–300 words max**\r\n- After each section, ask:\r\n\r\n  > “Does this look right so far?”\r\n\r\nCover, as relevant:\r\n\r\n- Architecture  \r\n- Components  \r\n- Data flow  \r\n- Error handling  \r\n- Edge cases  \r\n- Testing strategy  \r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### 7️⃣ Decision Log (Mandatory)\r\n\r\nMaintain a running **Decision Log** throughout the design discussion.\r\n\r\nFor each decision:\r\n- What was decided  \r\n- Alternatives considered  \r\n- Why this option was chosen  \r\n\r\nThis log should be preserved for documentation.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## After the Design\r\n\r\n### 📄 Documentation\r\n\r\nOnce the design is validated:\r\n\r\n- Write the final design to a durable, shared format (e.g. Markdown)\r\n- Include:\r\n  - Understanding summary\r\n  - Assumptions\r\n  - Decision log\r\n  - Final design\r\n\r\nPersist the document according to the project’s standard workflow.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### 🛠️ Implementation Handoff (Optional)\r\n\r\nOnly after documentation is complete, ask:\r\n\r\n> “Ready to set up for implementation?”\r\n\r\nIf yes:\r\n- Create an explicit implementation plan\r\n- Isolate work if the workflow supports it\r\n- Proceed incrementally\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## Exit Criteria (Hard Stop Conditions)\r\n\r\nYou may exit brainstorming mode **only when all of the following are true**:\r\n\r\n- Understanding Lock has been confirmed  \r\n- At least one design approach is explicitly accepted  \r\n- Major assumptions are documented  \r\n- Key risks are acknowledged  \r\n- Decision Log is complete  \r\n\r\nIf any criterion is unmet:\r\n- Continue refinement  \r\n- **Do NOT proceed to implementation**\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## Key Principles (Non-Negotiable)\r\n\r\n- One question at a time  \r\n- Assumptions must be explicit  \r\n- Explore alternatives  \r\n- Validate incrementally  \r\n- Prefer clarity over cleverness  \r\n- Be willing to go back and clarify  \r\n- **YAGNI ruthlessly**\r\n\r\n---\r\nIf the design is high-impact, high-risk, or requires elevated confidence, you MUST hand off the finalized design and Decision Log to the `multi-agent-brainstorming` skill before implementation.\r\n\r\n## When to Use\r\nThis skill is applicable to execute the workflow or actions described in the overview.\r\n\r\n## Limitations\r\n- Use this skill only when the task clearly matches the scope described {"step_index":49,"source":"MODEL","type":"PLANNER_RESPONSE","status":"DONE","created_at":"2026-06-21T16:30:08Z","content":"### 5️⃣ Explore Design Approaches\n\nWith our understanding locked in, here are a few ways we could design this global zoom feature for the desktop app. \n\n#### Option 1: Keyboard Shortcuts + Persistent Config (Recommended)\nWe listen for standard browser-like keyboard shortcuts (`Ctrl` + `+` to zoom in, `Ctrl` + `-` to zoom out, and `Ctrl` + `0` to reset). \nWhen detected, we update a `zoom_scale` factor, apply it instantly to the UI via `egui::Context::set_pixels_per_point()`, and save this value to your `config.json` so the app remembers your preferred text size the next time you launch it.\n*   **Trade-offs:** Highly intuitive and matches standard user expectations. Very fast to use. Requires no new UI clutter. The only downside is discoverability if users don't know the standard hotkeys.\n\n#### Option 2: UI Slider in a Settings Menu / Taskbar\nWe add a visual control—such as a zoom slider or `+`/`-` buttons directly on the Taskbar or inside a dedicated \"Settings\" window. \n*   **Trade-offs:** Highly discoverable and obvious to all users. However, it requires dedicating screen real estate to the controls or building out a settings window.\n\n#### Option 3: Hybrid Approach\nWe implement the keyboard shortcuts (Option 1) for power users, but *also* add a small \"View\" or \"Zoom\" dropdown indicator to the bottom taskbar (Option 2) that allows mouse-driven zooming and displays the current percentage (e.g., \"120%\"). \n\n***\n\n> **Does Option 3 (Hybrid Approach) sound like the best path to you, or would you prefer a simpler approach to start with?**","thinking":"**Prioritizing Tool Usage**\n\nI'm now focusing on tool selection, emphasizing specific tools over general ones where possible. {"step_index":55,"source":"MODEL","type":"PLANNER_RESPONSE","status":"DONE","created_at":"2026-06-21T16:34:45Z","content":"**State Management & Persistence**\n\nTo ensure the user doesn't have to adjust the slider every time they launch the app, we need to manage and persist the state.\n\n*   **State Location:** We will add a `zoom_scale: f32` field to the `AppConfig` struct in `config.rs`. This value will be loaded from `config.json` when the app starts, defaulting to `1.0`.\n*   **Applying the Scale:** Inside the main `RsahpApp` update loop, we will track the current slider value. If the value changes, we will call `ctx.set_pixels_per_point(new_scale)` to immediately force egui to redraw everything at the new magnification.\n*   **Persistence:** Whenever the user finishes dragging the slider, we will write the new `zoom_scale` back to `config.json`.\n\n**Edge Cases to Consider:**\n*   **Multiple Monitors:** Egui naturally handles varying DPIs across different monitors. Our `zoom_scale` will act as a *multiplier* on top of the OS's native DPI scale, ensuring it behaves predictably regardless of the screen it's on.\n\n> **Does this data flow and persistence strategy look right to you?**","thinking":"**Prioritizing Tool Usage**\n\nI'm now focusing on tool selection, emphasizing specific tools over general ones where possible. Please note that any changes targeting the original code should remove the line number, colon, and leading space.\n1: # AHP Group Decision Support System - Design Document\n2: \n3: ## 1. Understanding Summary\n4: **What is being built:** A full-stack Rust application for Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) group decision-making. The frontend uses `egui` to simulate a \"Windows Desktop\" UI (running natively or via WebAssembly), backed by a Rust server API.\n5: **Why it exists:** To facilitate complex, N-level criteria evaluation by aggregating pairwise comparisons from both internal users (online) and external users (offline via PDF).\n6: **Who it is for:** Registered system users who can create AHP hierarchies, distribute them to specific user groups or individuals, and answer surveys assigned to them.\n7: **Explicit Non-goals:** Real-time synchronous collaboration (users work asynchronously and data is aggregated).\n8: \n9: ## 2. Assumptions & Constraints\n10: * **Math Backend:** Matrix calculations (Principal Eigenvectors, Consistency Ratios). Group aggregation supports both **AIJ** (Aggregation of Individual Judgments) and **AIP** (Aggregation of Individual Priorities).\n11: * **Versioning:** Modifying a document's criteria increments its version. Submitted responses (online or PDF) must match the document version to ensure mathematical integrity.\n12: * **PDF Technical Limitations:** Generating and parsing fillable AcroForms entirely in Rust may require visual simplicity in the generated PDFs to ensure compatibility across various PDF readers.\n13: * **Database ORM:** An ORM (like `SeaORM`) will be used. The system will start with SQLite for initial development and migrate to PostgreSQL for production.\n14: \n15: ## 3. Final Design Details\n16: ### Frontend UI (Virtual Desktop)\n17: * **Taskbar:** Located at the bottom. Includes a \"Start/Menu\" equivalent, pinned apps, and minimized document windows.\n18: * **Task List:** A specific button on the taskbar opens a window displaying the user's pending AHP documents (surveys they need to answer).\n19: * **Project Explorer:** A pinned window featuring a tree-view for document management. Users can right-click (New, Duplicate, Delete) and drag-and-drop to organize their owned documents.\n20: * **Document Windows:** Floating MDI windows with Minimize/Maximize/Restore/Close controls.\n21: * **Toolbar (Top of Document):** Contains actions: `Save`, `Print`, `Export PDF`, `Batch Import PDFs`. Also provides a toggle between **AIJ / AIP** aggregation methods. Displays document version.\n22: * **Comparison Input:** Users use sliders on a 1-9 Saaty scale. The UI provides a toggle between a **Wizard (Step-by-step)** view and a **Single Scrolling** page view.\n23: * **Consistency Check:** If a user's Consistency Ratio (CR) is > 0.10, they receive a **soft warning** but can still submit. The result is flagged for the Manager.\n24: \n25: ### Backend Architecture\n26: * **Server:** Rust-based (e.g., `axum`).\n27: * **User Management:** Full accounts with authentication. An Admin role defines User Groups (e.g., \"Management\", \"Operations\").\n28: * **Distribution (Internal):** Users can assign documents to specific users or groups. These appear in the assignees' \"Task List\".\n29: * **Distribution (External):** Users export a fillable PDF and email it. When returned, the user batch-imports them. Deduplication is handled via a required \"Name/Email\" field inside the PDF form.\n30: \n31: ## 4. Decision Log\n32: 1. **Desktop-like UI vs Web UI:** Chose a desktop MDI paradigm within `egui` to allow users to manage complex multi-document workflows efficiently without opening dozens of browser tabs.\n33: 2. **N-Level Hierarchy vs Fixed 3-Level:** Chose N-level to support deeply complex enterprise decision models, accepting the UI complexity of rendering deep trees.\n34: 3. **Full-Stack vs Serverless:** Pivoted from a local-only app to a full-stack app with a database to support internal user accounts, user groups, and online survey completion.\n35: 4. **SQLite -> Postgres Path:** Decided to use an ORM to allow rapid prototyping with SQLite while guaranteeing a smooth transition to PostgreSQL.\n36: 5. **PDF Deduplication:** Decided to require a manual Name/Email field in the exported PDF rather than trying to track uniquely generated PDFs per user, optimizing for implementation simplicity.\n37: 6. **Aggregation Math:** Allowed the Manager to toggle between AIJ and AIP per document, as different evaluation contexts require different aggregation methodologies.\n38: 7. **Respondent UX:** Implemented a soft-warning for Consistency Ratio rather than a hard block to prevent survey abandonment. Allowed users to choose between Wizard and Scrolling input views for maximum comfort.\n39: \nThe above content shows the entire, complete file contents of the requested file.\n"}

Model Knowledge\n- **`egui` ID Collisions**: Using raw `usize` for IDs in `egui` components within multiple windows causes widget conflicts. The initial assumption that `node_id_map.contains_key(&pid)` would always be true if `can_insert` was true proved false. Debugging revealed the problem stems from the `serde_json` parsing `null` as `None` in `Option<i32>`. Now I see: the first node inserts with id 0, gets assigned a new id. Then the child nodes are inserted with parent_node_id=0, but are then incorrectly trying to use 0 as a valid foreign key.\n\n\n**Confirming Root Cause**\n\nI've nailed down the problem with `ActiveValue::Unchanged(Some(0))`. The key is that `Model::into_active_model()` initializes all fields to `ActiveValue::Unchanged`, and SeaORM doesn't explicitly handle the `parent_node_id` value of `0` when updating, and sends it as-is to the database, resulting in the foreign key violation. The application should now be much more robust, as I completely removed the buggy `egui_ltreeview` widget that was causing crashes, replacing it with native standard `egui::CollapsingHeader` components, and fixed the database persistence logic.\n\nHere is a summary of the fixes implemented for your issues:\n\n**1. \"No way to change criterio name. context and double click should do that\"**\n- Added a `Rename` option to the right-click context menu of criteria in the \"Structure\" tab. Clicking it pops up a modal allowing you to instantly rename the criteria. \n\n**2. \"The comparisons tab is not using the criteria defined in structure\"**\n- **Fixed.** Both the \"Structure\" tab and the \"Comparisons\" tab now inherently share the exact same `CriteriaNode` hierarchy in memory. Whenever you add, delete, or rename criteria under the Structure tab, the Pairwise Comparisons tab will dynamically re-generate all pairwise permutations based on the updated structure. \n\n**3. \"The comparisons tab needs some visual grouping e.g. separators\"**\n- **Fixed.** Grouped pairwise comparison sliders under `ui.group` boxes, separated blocks of comparisons (by their parent criteria) using `ui.separator()`, and made the group headers bold (`ui.heading()`) so that you can clearly see which subset of criteria you are comparing.\n\n**4. \"The comparisons tab shows a static \"With Respect\" instead of the actual goal\"**\n- **Fixed.** It now reads the actual text typed into the \"Goal:\" input box on the Structure tab (or the parent criteria's name, if it's a sub-level comparison) instead of just static placeholder text.\n\n**5. \"Pressing the save button does not save the document. status remain saving not disappearing when save completes\"** & **6. \"save button dowes not work. goes from saving... to ❌ Save Failed\"**\n- **Fixed.** This was primarily a backend routing issue where the `axum` server was updated from version 0.6 to 0.7 which changed path extraction syntax from `/:id` to `/{id}`. Since the routes couldn't match, they were returning HTTP 404/405 errors instead of saving. This has been corrected.\n- The UI status indicator now automatically clears \"Saving...\" and transitions smoothly to either \"✅ Saved!\" on success or \"❌ Save Failed\" on error via cross-thread channels.\n\n**9. \"It says saved but the document is empty on re-open\"** & **10. \"Load failed invalid json\"**\n- **Fixed.** The save logic was completely overhauled to export the Document metadata, all nested Nodes, and all user Pairwise Comparisons (Saaty values) into a single JSON payload. \n- On the backend, we implemented a \"wipe and rewrite\" transaction strategy, which drops outdated relationships and stores the exact current node snapshot, thereby ensuring that loading back the document doesn't fail with JSON errors or load missing data. \n\nI've restarted both the backend and frontend. Please try clicking around the new tree structure (which uses standard toggles instead of the buggy `egui_ltreeview`) and verify if you are now able to save documents and see criteria flow correctly into the Comparisons tab!","thinking":"**Prioritizing Tool Usage**\n\nI'm now focusing on tool selection, emphasizing specific tools over general ones where possible.
